coredata.dc:title: NH3, PH3, AsH3 adsorption and sensing on rare earth metal doped graphene: DFT insights
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Huijuan
Xu, Kexin
Gong, Zhijun
Li, Na
Zhang, Kai
Wu, Wenfei
coredata.dc:description: To explore new sensor materials towards hazardous gases NH3, PH3, and AsH3, their adsorptions on pristine graphene and rare earth metal (REM = La, Ce, Nd, Pm, Sm, Eu and Gd) doped graphenes were studied by using density functional theory calculations. The adsorption energies show that pristine graphene exhibits weak physisorption towards the NH3, PH3, and AsH3 gas molecules. Almost no changes appear on the geometrical structures, charge populations and DOSs of pristine graphene. However, the rare earth metal element modifications lead to enhanced chemisorption towards NH3 and weakened adsorption towards AsH3, accompanied with significant electron redistribution and orbital hybridization. The electrical conductivity of the graphenic substrate is also increased, leading to higher sensor response towards NH3. Facile sensitivity and recovery time reveals Ce doped graphene is an ideal candidate for room temperature monitoring of NH3.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NH3
PH3
AsH3
Rare earth metal doped graphene
Density functional theory calculations

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Binding energies, formation energies and adsorption energies
Geometrical structures of pristine and REM-doped graphenes and their gas adsorptions
Binding energies of REM-doped graphenes and adsorption energies of gas molecules adsorbed on pristine and REM-doped graphenes
Electronic properties of pristine and REM-doped graphenes and their adsystems

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology
Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has been supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region (Grant No. 2018LH05013 ) and the Innovation Fund Project of Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology (Grant No. 2017QDL-B16 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized structures of (a) pristine graphene, and (b) La-, (c) Ce-, (d) Nd-, (e) Pm-, (f) Sm-, (g) Eu-, and (h) Gd-doped graphenes. The gray sphere stands for C atom and the other colorful sphere in each figure stands for the respective rare-earth metal dopant.

Charge density graphs of the optimized structures of NH3 adsorption on (a) intrinsic, (b) La-, (c) Ce-, (d) Nd, (e) Pm-, (f) Sm-, (g) Eu- and (h) Gd-doped graphenes, PH3 adsorption on (i) intrinsic, (j) La-, (k) Ce-, (l) Nd, (m) Pm-, (n) Sm-, (o) Eu- and (p) Gd-doped graphenes and AsH3 adsorption on (q) intrinsic, (r) La-, (s) Ce-, (t) Nd, (u) Pm-, (v) Sm-, (w) Eu- and (x) Gd-doped graphenes. The isosurface is 0.15 electrons Å−3.

The bonding distance (BD) between graphene and the gas molecule of the (a) NH3, (b) PH3, (c) AsH3 adsystems, and (d) the deviance of BD relative to Lcov . Lcov represents the theoretical covalent radii. Lvdw represents the theoretical van der waals radii.
(a) Eb of REM doped graphenes and (b) |Eads| of the IG, LaG, CeG, NdG, PmG, SmG, EuG and GdG adsystems of NH3, PH3 and AsH3.

Difference charge density graphs of NH3 on (a) IG, (b) LaG, (c) CeG, (d) NdG, (e) PmG, (f) SmG, (g) EuG, and (h) GdG, PH3 on (i) IG, (j) LaG, (k) CeG, (l) NdG, (m) PmG, (n) SmG, (o) EuG, and (p) GdG, and AsH3 on (q) IG, (r) LaG, (s) CeG, (t) NdG, (u) PmG, (v) SmG, (w) EuG, and (x) GdG. Blue color indicates electron accumulation region and yellow color indicates electron depletion region. The isosurface is set as 0.002 e Å−3.
DOSs of NH3, PH3, AsH3 adsorptions on pristine (a), La- (b), Ce- (c), Nd- (d), Pm- (e), Sm- (f), Eu- (g) and Gd- (i) doped graphenes.

DOS of the linking atoms in rare earth doped graphenes and adsorbed gases in the (a) NH3/IG, (b) PH3/IG, (c) AsH3/IG, (d) NH3/LaG, (e) PH3/LaG, (f) AsH3/LaG, (g) NH3/CeG, (h) PH3/CeG, (i) AsH3/CeG, (j) NH3/NdG, (k) PH3/NdG, (l) AsH3/NdG, (m) NH3/PmG, (n) PH3/PmG, (o) AsH3/PmG, (p) NH3/SmG, (q) PH3/SmG, (r) AsH3/SmG, (s) NH3/EuG, (t) PH3/EuG, (u) AsH3/EuG and (v) NH3/GdG, (w) PH3/GdG, (x) AsH3/GdG adsystems.
The sensor responses of IG, LaG, CeG, NdG, PmG, SmG, EuG and GdG towards NH3, PH3, AsH3 molecules.
The recovery time (unit: s) of the (a) NH3, (b) PH3 and (c) AsH3 adsystems.

Selected geometrical parameters and binding distances (BDs) of gas molecules adsorbed on intrinsic and REM-doped (REM = Be, Mg, Ca or Sr) graphenes. L stands for the C atom of intrinsic graphene or the Be, Mg, Ca, or Sr metal atom that was doped into graphene. C1, C2 and C3 are three nearest carbon atoms around the dopant site.

Adsorption energies (Eads) and Mulliken charge population (e) of pristine and REM-doped graphene adsystems. L represents the linking C atom in pristine graphene or the linking REM atom in REM doped graphenes. C1, C2, C3 represent the three carbon atoms around the linking L atom. XH3 (X = N, P or As) represents the adsorbed gas molecule. The positive or negative value of Mulliken charge indicates electron loss or accumulation compared to normal electron distribution of the isolated atom.

head.ce:title: NH3, PH3, AsH3 adsorption and sensing on rare earth metal doped graphene: DFT insights

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Environment, Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology, Baotou 014010, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy and Environment, Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology, Baotou 014010, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Efficient and Clean Combustion, Baotou 014010, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Efficient and Clean Combustion

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Efficient and Clean Combustion, Baotou 014010, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Energy and Environment, Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology, Baotou 014010, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To explore new sensor materials towards hazardous gases NH3, PH3, and AsH3, their adsorptions on pristine graphene and rare earth metal (REM = La, Ce, Nd, Pm, Sm, Eu and Gd) doped graphenes were studied by using density functional theory calculations. The adsorption energies show that pristine graphene exhibits weak physisorption towards the NH3, PH3, and AsH3 gas molecules. Almost no changes appear on the geometrical structures, charge populations and DOSs of pristine graphene. However, the rare earth metal element modifications lead to enhanced chemisorption towards NH3 and weakened adsorption towards AsH3, accompanied with significant electron redistribution and orbital hybridization. The electrical conductivity of the graphenic substrate is also increased, leading to higher sensor response towards NH3. Facile sensitivity and recovery time reveals Ce doped graphene is an ideal candidate for room temperature monitoring of NH3.

ce:section.ce:para: The hydrides of Group V elements including NH3, PH3, and AsH3 have substantial impact on environmental and health issues of human life·NH3 is a strong irritant, colorless and flammable gas. Irritation of the respiratory system, eye, lung and skin would occur after exposure to NH3. The permitted maximum concentration of NH3 vapor is 20 ppm in the workplace [1]. PH3 is a colourless, flammable highly toxic gas. It could act on the central nervous system, respiratory system, kidney and liver. The exposure limit is 0.3 ppm [2]. AsH3 is a kind of colourless, flammable gas with severe hemolytic toxicity. Acute intravascular hemolysis could be caused by inhalation of high concentration AsH3 in a short period of time [3]. The toxicity threshold level value is only 0.005 ppm. To bring more safety assurance to human's life, effectively monitoring NH3, PH3, and AsH3 is of important significance. High-sensitivity NH3, PH3 and AsH3 sensors, which can convert the concentration of gases to electrical signal for detection, have attracted substantial interest.

The development of appropriate sensing materials for the NH3, PH3 and AsH3 gases is of great significance. Md. Shahzad Khan’s research shows that ZnS nanotubes are sensitive to NH3 and PH3 [4]. V2O5 (001) is also a good candidate as a hazardous gas nanosensor [5]. Research has shown that indicate the sensitivity about chemical gas such as AsH3, PH3 and NH3 adsorptions sensors can be enhanced greatly for single-walled carbon nanotubes with transition metal doping. A novel bismuthene nanotube, which can be used to detection gas molecules such as PH3, NO2 and NH3 successfully, also was studied by replacing a C atom by a Cr, V, Ti or Sc atom on the armchair single-walled carbon nanotube [6]. The first graphene-based ammonia gas sensor was reported by Andre Geim’s group employing the micromechanical cleavage of graphite on an oxidized silicon substrate in 2007 [7]. The sensitivity of graphene-based chemical gas sensors toward NH3 could be drastically improved by introducing the Pd dopants [8]. In our previous research, AsH3, PH3 and NH3 adsorptions on Be, Mg, Ca and Sr doped graphenes are investigated [9]. Mg doped graphene exhibits good performance in terms of sensitivity, reversibility and work temperature towards PH3 and AsH3. Attributed to the distinct structure and excellent electrical conductivity, investigation on the sensing ability of graphene and its derivants is increasing.

Rare-earth metal doping is an effective method to improve the performances of various materials [10–16]. Experimental studies have shown that Bi2WO6 doped with rare earth ions (La, Ce, Gd, and Yb) exhibited enhanced removal efficiency of RhB under simulated solar irradiation due to speculiar 4f orbits [10]. The phosphate adsorption capacity of biochar [11] could be greatly improved after Ce or La doping, especially La.Gd doped carbon nanodots (CDs) [12] with uniform particle size of 3–4 nm as MRI/FI bimodal probes were prepared in 2012. Hasan et al fabricated Nd3+-doped biocompatible graphene quantum dots [13], which exhibit NIR fluorescence at approximate to 1060 attributed to intrinsic transitions of Nd3+. Ce-doped CDs [14] with graphite-like structure were synthesized, showing hydroxyl radical scavenging capacity. Density functional theory studies show that La and Ce doping could modulate carbon atom adsorption on the ferrum surface [15]. At present, the effect of rare-earth metal doping on graphene’s gas adsorption and sensing performance is rarely studied [17,18]. However, compared to our previous research [9], the unique charge transfer influenced by the unpaired 4f orbits and appropriate adsorption strength attributed weak bound ability of outer 4f valence electrons may facilitate novel sensing materials with facile work temperature.

Density functional theory calculations are increasingly used to explore novel physicochemical properties [19,20], gas sensing materials [8,9], rate-limiting catalytic steps [21] etc.. In this work, we performed density functional theory calculations to investigate the NH3, PH3 and AsH3 sensing ability of REM (REM = La, Nd, Pm, Ce, Sm, Gd and Eu) doped graphene. Geometrical structures, adsorption energies, orbital hybridization and electron transfer of the adsystems are thoroughly analyzed. The sensor responses and recovery capabilities are also evaluated. The results of the present study could facilitate exploration of the potential use of rare earth doping in hazardous gas sensors.

Pristine graphene supercell (6 × 6 × 1) is first built for investigation of NH3, PH3, AsH3 adsorptions before rare earth metal modifications. A large supercell could advoid the lateral interactions between gas molecules, and the interactions between neighboring graphene layers.

Through substituting one C atom with a rare earth metal dopant (REM = La, Ce, Nd, Pm, Sm, Eu or Gd), the corresponding rare earth metal doped graphene could be built, with a doping concentration of 1.39%. In the following sections, La, Ce, Nd, Pm, Sm, Eu and Gd-doped graphene are signified as LaG, CeG, NdG, PmG, SmG, EuG and GdG, respectively. IG and REG are used to represent pristine graphene and rare earth metal doped graphene, respectively.

With respect to gas adsorptions, the NH3, PH3, AsH3 molecules are initially docked onto the carbon atom of IG or the rare earth metal dopant of REG through the N, P, and As linking atoms vertically, respectively. The adsorption height is 2.5 Å. For simplicity, the NH3/REG, PH3/REG and AsH3/REG adsystems are abbreviated as N-REG, P-REG, and As-REG (RE = La, Ce, Nd, Pm, Sm, Eu or Gd), respectively.

Density functional theory calculations are carried out using the DMol3 code [22]. The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) generalized gradient approximation (GGA) is adopted to describe the exchange–correlation energies. Spin unrestricted conditions are performed under the double numerical polarization basis set. 6 × 6 × 1 k grid is adopted. 10-6 a.u. of energy is used as the the self-consistent field calculation tolerance. 10-5 a.u of energy, 10-3 a.u of gradient and 10-3 a.u. of displacement are used as the structural optimization tolerance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding energies, formation energies and adsorption energies
ce:section.ce:para:
The binding energies (Eb ) of REGs are calculated as [23]:
(1)
E b
=
E
REG
-
E
REM
-
E
SV
where EREG denotes the total energy of REG, EREM denotes the energy of the corresponding rare earth metal atom and ESV denotes the single-vacancy graphene, respectively.

The adsorption energies (Eads ) of NH3, PH3 and AsH3 on pristine graphene and REGs are calculated as [21,24]:
(2)
E ads
=
E
X H 3 / G
-
E G
+
E
X H 3
where EXH3/G, EG and EXH3 denote the energies of the XH3/G adsystems, the graphenic adsorption substrate and the adsorbed hazardous NH3, PH3 or AsH3 molecule.

The sensitivity is evaluated as [25,26]:
(3)
S =
N F
( g )
-
N F
( a )
N F
( a )
× 100 %
NF (g) and NF (a) denote DOS data around the Fermi level with and without adsorbed NH3, PH3 or AsH3 molecule, respectively.

Evaluation of the recovery time is performed through the Van’t-Hoff-Arrhenius formula [27]:
(4)
τ =
A
- 1
e
( -
E a
/ B T )
where A denotes the apparent frequency factor (1012 s−1), T denotes the working temperature, B denotes Boltzmann constant, and Ea denotes the desorption energy barrier. It is usually assumed that the value of Ea equals the value of Eads , since desorption is the reverse process of adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometrical structures of pristine and REM-doped graphenes and their gas adsorptions
ce:section.ce:para: With respect to pristine graphene, the CC bonding lengths are 1.42 Å, and all the bonding angles are 120° (Fig. 1 (a)), which are consistent with published results. Regarding rare earth metal doped graphenes (Fig. 1(b-h)), obvious geometrical deformations appear. The bonding length of the three REM-C bonds are in the range of 2.21–2.41 Å, much less than the sum of the atomic covalent radii [28] of the corresponding REM and C atom, indicating strong chemical binding of the REM dopant. Due to larger atomic radius of the REM dopant [28], outward protrusion of REM dopants and neighbouring graphenic carbon atoms occurs on the surface of graphene. The formation of the protrusion structure would contribute to reduction in the spatial impedance and improvement in the adsorption ability. Attributed to outer shell electron configurations, CeSV and NdSV exhibit Jahn-Teller-like effects, with two equal REMC bonds.

For NH3 (Fig. 2 (a)), PH3 (Fig. 2(i)), and AsH3 (Fig. 2(q)) adsorptions on pristine graphene, the CC bond lengths and the CCC bond angles of pristine graphene remain almost unchanged (Table 1 ). The bonding distances between NH3, PH3, and AsH3 and the pristine graphene plane were 3.22, 3.53 and 3.52 Å, respectively. The bonding distances are much larger than theoretical covalent bonding lengths (Fig. 3 (a-c)). Thus pristine graphene exhibits very weak physisorption interactions towards the three gas molecules.

For NH3 (Fig. 2(b-h)), PH3 (Fig. 2(j-p)), and AsH3 (Fig. 2(r-x)) adsorptions on REM-doped graphenes, NH3, PH3, and AsH3 dockings significantly changed the CREM bonding lengths and the CREMC bonding angles of REM-doped graphenes (Table 1). The binding distances between NH3 and the REM dopant range from 2.77 Å to 3.04 Å (Fig. 3(a)). Relative to the theoretical REM-N covalent radii, the respective deviation defined as (BD-Lcov)/Lcov is in the range of 0.73–9.35 % (Fig. 3(d)). For PH3, the bonding distances range from 3.27 Å to 3.53 Å (Fig. 3(b)). Except for CeG and GdG, the respective deviation of the bonding distance relative to the theoretical REM-P covalent radius is in the range of 10.78–13.59% (Fig. 3(d)). With respect to CeG (9.65%) and GdG (7.57%), the deviations are within 10%. Regarding AsH3, the bonding distances range from 3.34 Å to 3.74 Å (Fig. 3(c)). Except for LaG, EuG and GdG, the respective deviation of the bonding distance relative to the theoretical REM-P covalent radius is in the range of 10.03–15.79% (Fig. 3(d)). Regarding LaG, EuG and GdG, the deviations are 9.82%, 9.43% and 5.70%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding energies of REM-doped graphenes and adsorption energies of gas molecules adsorbed on pristine and REM-doped graphenes
ce:section.ce:para: With respect to the binding energies (Fig. 4 (a)) of REM doped graphenes, a non-monotonic trend is observed. LaG exhibits the largest binding energy, and the value −6.61 eV is close to literature report [17]. EuG exhibits the weakest binding ability, with a Eb value of −3.53 eV. All of these combinations are exothermic, indicating the feasibility of the corresponding doped structure. Meanwhile, the cohesive energies [29] of rare earth metal are 4.47, 4.32, 3.4, 2.14, 1.86 and 4.14 eV/atom for La, Ce, Nd, Sm, Eu and Gd. The binding strength of rare earth metal doping is much stronger than the corresponding cohesive strength. Therefore, the rare earth atom could be stably mobilized without formation of clusters.

For NH3, PH3 and AsH3 adsorptions on the surface of pristine graphene (Table 2 ), the adsorption energies are −0.11 eV, −0.18 eV and −0.45 eV, respectively. Thus pristine graphene exhibits weak physisorption towards NH3, PH3 and AsH3. Physisorption is also called van der waals adsorption, which is caused by the van der waals forces between the adsorbed gas and the substrate [30]. For van der waals interactions, larger molecular mass means stronger attraction force, especially for the adsorbed molecules with a similar structure. Therefore, the adsorption ability of three gas molecules on pristine graphene is in the order AsH3 > PH3 > NH3.

After REM doping, the adsorption energies of NH3 on REM doped graphenes are: NH3/CeG (−0.82 eV) > NH3/GdG (−0.69 eV) > NH3/EuG (−0.67 eV) > NH3/SmG (−0.62 eV) > NH3/NdG (−0.61 eV) > NH3/PmG (−0.60 eV) > NH3/LaG (−0.47 eV). The adsorption energies of PH3 on REM doped graphenes are: PH3/CeG(−0.28 eV) = PH3/GdG(−0.28 eV) > PH3/EuG (−0.22 eV) > PH3/NdG (−0.21 eV) > PH3/PmG (−0.20 eV) > PH3/SmG (−0.20 eV) > PH3/LaG (−0.12 eV). The adsorption energies of AsH3 on REM doped graphenes are: AsH3/GdG (−0.18 eV) > AsH3/NdG (−0.14 eV) > AsH3/PmG (−0.13 eV) = AsH3/EuG (−0.13 eV) > AsH3/SmG (−0.11 eV) > AsH3/CeG (−0.07 eV) > AsH3/LaG (−0.05 eV). The adsorption ability of three gas molecules on graphene doped by the rare earth metal atoms is all in the order NH3 > PH3 > AsH3. In the warping structure of the REM doped graphenic substrate, the REM dopant in the warped graphenic substrate mainly interacts with the adsorbed molecules. Attributed to the unpaired 4f orbital, the REM dopants act as lewis acid sites, which could easily accept electrons. Then the basicity of the three gas molecules in the order NH3 > PH3 > AsH3 determines the adsorption strengths.

Through comparing of the adsorption energies of NH3, PH3 and AsH3. molecules on pristine graphene and REM doped graphenes, it is found that REM modifications could enhance the adsorption strength of NH3 on graphene and suppress the adsorption strength of AsH3, while nearly no influence on PH3, compared to pristine graphene (Fig. 4(b)). The finding is out of the general expectations that metal doping usually lead to good molecules adsorption capability [31]. REM doping could only lead to enhanced adsorption strength towards NH3, while PH3 and AsH3 could not be strongly adsorbed. The phenomenon is attributed to the transition of the adsorption mechanism. With respect to adsorption on pristine graphene, van der waals interactions dominate, greatly influenced by gas mass. With respect to adsorption on REM doped graphenes, interactions between lewis acid and lewis alkali dominate, which is greatly influenced by the basicity of the three gases. Compared to literature reports, gas adsorptions on REM doped graphenes exhibit weaker adsorption strengths. This may be related to the more electronic shells of REM atoms, exhibiting less ability to bind the outer valence electron.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electronic properties of pristine and REM-doped graphenes and their adsystems
ce:section.ce:para: With respect to NH3 docking on prinstine graphene, the linking N and C atom exhibit significant electron reduction (Fig. 5 (a)). Mild electron accumulation is found on N at the hydrogen side. The partial charge transfer (PCT) from NH3 to pristine graphene is 0.007 e. Regarding NH3 docking on La-doped graphene (Fig. 5(b)), electron loss occurs on the LaG surface and the three H atoms of the NH3 molecule. Electron accumulation occurs between La and N facilitating formation of the La-N binding. The significant electron redistribution exhibits strong interactions between La and N atoms. The PCT between NH3 and La is 0.100 e. Similar electron rearrangement appears when NH3 is adsorbed at the Ce, Nd, Pm, Sm, Eu, Gd-doped graphenes (Fig. 5(c-h)). The PCTs (Table 2) between NH3 and Ce, Nd, Pm, Sm, Eu, Gd-doped graphenes were 0.128, 0.099, 0.101, 0.079, 0.085 and 0.095 e, respectively.

For PH3 adsorption on prinstine graphene (Fig. 5(b)), only electron reduction is found on the surface of the graphenic carbon substrate. The PCT between PH3 and IG is 0.010 e. For the PH3/LaG adystem (Fig. 5(e)), La exhibits significant electron loss at the PH3 side and mild electron gain at the graphenic carbon side. The surrounding carbon atoms exhibit mild electron gain. Other carbon atoms are unaffected by the PH3 adsorption. The PH3 molecule exhibit electron loss. An electron accumulation area appears between PH3 and LaG facilitating formation of the La-P binding. Similar electron redistribution appears for PH3 adsorption (Fig. 5(k-p)) on the Ce, Nd, Pm, Sm, Eu, Gd-doped graphenes and the AsH3/REG adsytems (Fig. 5(q-x)). The charge transfers (Table 2) between PH3 and La-, Ce-, Nd-, Pm-, Sm-, Eu- and Gd-doped graphenes are 0.104, 0.131, 0.117, 0.109, 0.086, 0.102 and 0.118 e. The charge transfers (Table 2) between AsH3 and La-, Ce-, Nd-, Pm-, Sm-, Eu- and Gd-doped graphenes are 0.142, 0.094, 0.164, 0.160, 0.111, 0.129 and 0.147 e, respectively.

In comparison to prinstine graphene, REM doping leads to significant electron redistribution in the adsystems. Both the linking N, P, As and rare earth metal atoms contribute electrons to form N, P or As-REM linking, regardless of the adsorption strengths. REM doping also significantly excites the donor ability of the gas molecules as lewis acid sites. With respect to physisorption, negiligible charge transfer between the adsorbed gas and the substrate occurs. Attributed to the transition of physisorption to chemisorption, the electron transfers from the NH3, PH3 or AsH3 molecule to the REM-doped graphene are one order larger.

To obtain further understanding of the electronic properties, the DOSs of the pristine graphene and REM-doped graphenes adsystems were calculated and plotted (Fig. 6 ). The DOSs of pristine graphene were slightly changed by gas adsorption. Mild changes in electronic properties indicate mild electronic current changes and slightly sensible effect to NH3, PH3 and AsH3. However, the DOSs of REM-doped graphenes are greatly changed after NH3, PH3 and AsH3 gas adsorptions. These changes in the DOSs, especially in the region near the Fermi level, are expected to result in obvious changes in the corresponding electronic properties. This is very beneficial for gas sensing applications. Thus, it could be concluded that the REM-doped graphenes are more suitable for NH3, PH3, and AsH3 sensing than pristine graphene.

As for NH3 adsorption on IG (Fig. 7 (a)), the N 2p orbital of NH3 exhibits two peaks centered around −6.5 and −1.5 eV. The C 2p orbital exhibits no changes after NH3 adsorption, in consistentce with weak interactions. With respect to NH3 adsorption on LaG (Fig. 7(d)), part electron of the La 5d orbital hybridizes with the N 2p orbital around −2.0 eV, while part electron of the La 5d orbital moves to the low energy region and hybridize with the N 2p orbital around −4.2 eV. The N 2p orbital exhibits great changes compared to the NH3/CG systems. For the NH3/CeG adsystem (Fig. 7(g)), obvious orbital hybridization appears around −5.0 eV between the N 2p orbital and the 4f orbital of the Ce dopant. For the NH3/NdG, NH3/PmG and NH3/SmG adsystems (Fig. 7(j, m, p)), obvious orbital hybridization around −5.0 eV and −2.3 eV between the N 2p orbital and the 4f orbital of the Nd, Pm and Sm dopant. For the NH3/EuG (Fig. 7(s)) and NH3/GdG (Fig. 7(v)) adsystems, mild orbital hybridization happen around −5.4 eV and −5.0 eV between the N 2p orbital and the 4f orbital of the Eu and Gd dopants.

With respect to PH3 adsorptions on IG (Fig. 7(b)), the C 2p orbital exhibits no changes after PH3 adsorption, in consistence with weak interactions. For the PH3/LaG adsystem (Fig. 7(e)), the La 5d orbital moves to the low-energy region and forms three peaks around −0.4, −2.1 and −4.3 eV. Compared to the P 3p orbital in PH3/IG, the P 3p orbital in PH3/LaG exhibits significant electron redistribution. Above the fermi level, the electron centers around 2.15 eV. Below the fermi level, the electron centers around −0.4 eV, −2 eV, −4.2 eV and −6.8 eV. The orbital overlap between La 5d and P 3p occurs around −0.4 eV, −2 eV, −4.2 eV and −6.8 eV. With respect to PH3 adsorption on CeG (Fig. 7(h)), orbital overlap between the 4f orbital of the rare earth metal dopant and the 3p orbital of P occurs around −4.2 eV and −6.8 eV. With respect to PH3 adsorption on NdG, PmG, SmG, EuG and GdG (Fig. 7(k, n, q, t w)), orbital overlap between the 4f orbital of the rare earth metal dopant and the 3p orbital of P occurs around −2 eV, −4.2 eV and −6.8 eV. Similar orbital hybridizations occur for the AsH3 adsorptions on pristine graphene (Fig. 7(c)) and rare earth metal doped graphenes (Fig. 7(f, i, l, o, r, u, h)).

According to Eq. (3), the sensor responses of all the adsystems are calculated using the DOS data around the Fermi level before and after NH3, PH3, and AsH3 dockings (Fig. 6). The sensitivity is exhibited in Fig. 8 and the concrete data are presented in Table S1.

For pristine graphene, DOSs at the Fermi level exhibit almost no changes after the docking of NH3 and PH3 (Fig. 6(a)). However, mild reduction appears after AsH3 adsorption. Thus pristine graphene possesses the best sensitivity (17.40%) towards AsH3. With respect to LaG, negligible changes appear in the DOSs at Fermi level (Fig. 6(b)), and the sensitivity is all below 7.50%. For CeG, NH3, PH3, and AsH3 adsorptions lead to obvious reduction of the DOSs at Fermi level (Fig. 6(c)). And the sensitivity ranges from 13.06% to 40.44%. For NdG, the sensitivity towards NH3 is 8.14% and the sensitivity towards PH3 and AsH3 is ngeligible. For PmG, the sensor response is in the range of 8.61–12.89%. For SmG, the sensitivity towards NH3 and PH3 is 23.53% and 22.15%, and the sensitivity towards AsH3 is 5.91%. For EuG, the sensitivity towards NH3 is 25.62%, and the sensitivity towards PH3 and AsH3 is about 14.30%. For GdG, the sensitivity towards NH3 is 11.13%, and the sensitivity towards PH3 and AsH3 is below 5%.

Compared to pristine graphene, the rare earth metal (La, Ce, Nd, Pm, Sm, Eu, Gd) doped graphenes all exhibit higher sensitivity towards NH3. Among them, CeG and EuG could be available for NH3 sensing. With respect to PH3 sensing, Ce, Pm, Sm, Eu modifications lead to higher sensitivity relative to pristine graphene. For AsH3, DOS changes at the Fermi level caused by the rare earth metal doping are very small, with negligible sensitivity relative to pristine graphene.

To explore the application potential of a gas sensor, thorough evaluation of the adsorption and desorption behavior of the adsystems is necessary. The binding distances of NH3, PH3 and AsH3 to REM-doped graphenes are all larger than the sum of corresponding covalent radii (Fig. 4). Therefore, the REM doped graphenes exhibit a not very strong adsorption interaction towards the XH3 (X = N, P, As) molecules. It is therefore predictable that simple heating processing is facile to desorb the gas molecules from our proposed material. Meanwhile, recovery of the surface of the REM doped graphenes could be realized.

The recovery time (τ) is a common parameter to evaluate the time needed for a gas molecule’s desorption from the sensing material’s surface. To achieve a full understanding, the desorption properties of the various adsystems at 298 K, 398 K, 498 K and 548 K are calculated. The desorption time is exhibited in Fig. 9 and Table S2·NH3 on LaG, NdG, PmG, SmG, EuG, GdG could be desorbed at 298 K or above within 100 ms. Nevertheless, NH3 desorption on CeG needs 73.04 s at 298 K, and 20 ms at 398 K. PH3 and AsH3 could be desorbed from all the REM doped graphenes at 298 K or above with the recovery time around several nano-seconds. Since very short recovery time is unsuitable for gas detection [32], CeG seems appropriate for NH3 sensing attributed to the facile recovery time. Considering the sensitivity and recovery time, Ce doepd graphene could be a potential candidate for room temperature NH3 sensing application.

To explore new sensor materials, the adsorptions of NH3, PH3, and AsH3 on pristine graphene and rare earth metal (REM = La, Ce, Nd, Pm, Sm, Eu and Gd) doped graphenes are studied by performing DFT calculations. Pristine graphene exhibits physisorption interactions towards NH3, PH3 and AsH3. The dockings of NH3, PH3 and AsH3 lead to negligible changes in geometrical structures, charge populations and DOSs. However, rare earth metal element modifications lead to enhanced chemisorption towards NH3 and weakened adsorption towards AsH3, accompanied with significant electron redistribution and orbital hybridization. The electrical conductivity of the graphenic substrate is also increased, leading to higher sensor response towards NH3. With facile sensitivity and recovery time, Ce doped graphene seems a potential candidate for room temperature monitoring of NH3 molecules. Our study would advance the application of rare earth metal doped graphene in gas sensing fields.

Huijuan Luo: Conceptualization. Kexin Xu: Investigation. Zhijun Gong: Project administration. Na Li: Writing - original draft. Kai Zhang: Writing - review & editing. Wenfei Wu: Supervision, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region (Grant No. 2018LH05013) and the Innovation Fund Project of Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology (Grant No. 2017QDL-B16).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2021.150390.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
